pageSubtitle: v0.6.5 Vendor-Stratified Model — 9 active features, Optuna TPE, honest generalization

overview.description: RUBLI v0.6.5 scores every contract with a statistical risk indicator using per-sector logistic regression trained on ~748 documented cases (~603 vendors). Scores measure similarity to known corruption patterns — the model uses vendor-stratified validation (no vendor in both train and test).
overview.oecdBenchmark: OECD extended range for risk-indicator systems

riskLevels.title: Risk Levels
riskLevels.disclaimer: Risk scores are statistical indicators with 95% confidence intervals. A high score indicates similarity to documented corruption patterns — it does not constitute proof.

coefficients.title: v0.6.5 Global Model Coefficients
coefficients.subtitle: 16 z-score features (9 active in v0.6.5) ranked by absolute coefficient magnitude. Positive = increases risk; negative = decreases risk.

keyFindings.title: What the Model Learned
keyFindings.concentrationDetail: Price volatility (+0.53) and vendor concentration (+0.37) are the top 2 predictors. These reflect Mexico's documented corruption pattern: large vendors with variable contract sizes capturing institutional monopolies.
keyFindings.directAwardDetail: The direct_award coefficient is +0.031 in v0.6.5, confirming the v5.1 sign correction. Direct awards are consistently associated with documented corruption cases in Mexican procurement.
keyFindings.singleBidDetail: Single bidding is regularized to exactly 0.000 in v0.6.5 across all 13 models. Known-bad vendors tend to win through direct awards rather than single-bid competitive procedures, so single bidding provides no discriminating power in our ground truth.
keyFindings.coBiddingZeroDetail: Regularized to exactly 0.000 in all 13 models. The training cases involve concentration, not collusion rings.

validation.title: Ground Truth Validation
validation.description: Detection rates on ~748 documented corruption cases (~603 vendors, ~288K contracts across all 12 sectors).
validation.footnote: * Case 9 shares vendors with Case 5 (Odebrecht). Documented for reference.

statisticalMethods.zScoreDesc: Each feature is standardized relative to its sector and year baseline. A direct award in Defensa (80% are direct) is less suspicious than in Educación (50% are direct). Z-scores capture this context.
statisticalMethods.mahalanobisDesc: Measures how unusual a contract's feature vector is in multidimensional space. Replaces the 5 hardcoded interaction pairs from v3.3 with a full covariance matrix that captures all pairwise relationships.
statisticalMethods.perSectorDesc: v0.6.5 trains 12 dedicated models (one per sector) plus a global fallback. Optuna-tuned ElasticNet (C=0.0100, l1_ratio=0.9673, near-pure L1) with vendor-stratified 70/30 split.
statisticalMethods.perSectorFormula: S(z) = σ(β₀ + βᵀz) / c, where c = 0.300 (PU correction)

v33.title: Previous Model: v3.3 (Weighted Checklist)
v33.description: v3.3 used 8 expert-weighted factors based on IMF CRI methodology. Preserved in risk_score_v3 column for comparison.

dataSources.title: Data Sources — COMPRANET
dataSources.description: Data quality varies significantly by period. Risk scores for 2002–2010 are directional estimates with lower reliability.

pageSubline: Statistical analysis of 3.05M Mexican government procurement contracts

groundTruth.pageSubtitle: Documented corruption cases validate the risk detection model
groundTruth.callout: This page measures RUBLI's detection capability against real, documented Mexican procurement corruption cases. Each case was independently investigated by journalists, auditors, or prosecutors. Our model never saw these labels during training — the detection rates shown here represent genuine out-of-sample predictive performance.
groundTruth.liveDetectionRates: Live detection rates computed from current risk scores. Click a row to expand.
groundTruth.noCasesFound: No ground truth cases found in the database.
groundTruth.failedSummary: Failed to load summary data. Please try refreshing.
groundTruth.factorLiftDesc: How much more often each risk factor appears in documented corruption cases vs the general contract population. Lift > 1.0 = over-represented in known-bad contracts.
groundTruth.modelComparisonDesc: Detection rate comparison across model versions (v3.3, v4.0, v0.6.5). High+ = score >= 0.30, Critical = score >= 0.50.
groundTruth.noModelComparison: No model comparison data available. Run validate_risk_model.py to generate results.

modelTransparency.disclaimer: Risk scores are statistical indicators, not legal conclusions. High scores indicate similarity to documented corruption patterns — they do not constitute proof.
modelTransparency.subtitleLive: Understanding how the {{version}} risk scoring model works — coefficients, validation, and limitations
modelTransparency.bannerLive: Coefficient chart and model comparison are loaded from the live API. Use the sector selector to view per-sector model weights.
modelTransparency.bannerFallback: Falling back to static documentation data. Live endpoints will be used when available.

coeffChart.title: What Drives Risk Scores?
coeffChart.description: Logistic regression coefficients — larger bars mean stronger influence on the risk score
coeffChart.callout: Each coefficient represents the change in the risk indicator's log-odds per 1 standard deviation increase in the z-scored feature. Positive values increase the risk score; negative values decrease it. Coefficients measure statistical association with documented corruption patterns, not literal probability of corruption.
coeffChart.calloutCI: Error bars show 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (1,000 resamples).

perSector.title: Per-Sector Models
perSector.description: Top 3 factors for each of the 12 per-sector logistic regression sub-models
perSector.fallbackNote: Sector models are applied when the contract's sector has 30+ contracts in the training data. Global model is used as fallback.

comparison.title: Model Comparison: v3.3 vs v0.6.5
comparison.description: The vendor-stratified framework (v0.6.5) dramatically outperforms the weighted checklist (v3.3) on every metric

perCase.title: Per-Case Detection Performance
perCase.description: Detection performance on ~748 documented corruption cases — foundation 22 cases shown below with matched vendor contracts
perCase.callout: Detection % measures the share of contracts from each case that the model flags at medium risk or above (score >= {{medium}}). High+ % measures the share flagged at high or critical levels (score >= {{high}}). Cases with more contracts provide stronger validation signal.

limitations.warning: No model is perfect. These limitations are disclosed for transparency. A high risk score indicates statistical anomaly consistent with corruption patterns — it does not constitute proof of wrongdoing.
limitations.groundTruthConcDesc: v0.6.5 expanded to ~748 cases and ~603 vendors with a per-vendor cap of 100 training contracts. While this reduces the dominance of mega-cases, Salud and Agricultura sectors still contribute disproportionately due to the IMSS, Segalmex, and DICONSA/LICONSA ecosystems.
limitations.smallCaseDesc: Cases with few contracts have lower detection rates: La Estafa Maestra (10 contracts, 0% high+), Oceanografia (2 contracts, 50% detection). The model requires sufficient contract volume to detect patterns reliably.
limitations.noCausalDesc: A high risk score indicates a statistical anomaly consistent with corruption patterns observed in ~748 documented cases. It does not prove corruption. Some sectors (defense, energy) have structural reasons for high vendor concentration that are legitimate.
limitations.dataQualityDesc: Structure A data (2002-2010) has only 0.1% RFC coverage, making vendor identification unreliable for that period. Z-scores and risk scores for earlier contracts may be less accurate. Network analysis is most reliable for 2010+ data where RFC coverage exceeds 15%.

pipeline.title: How the Score is Computed
pipeline.description: End-to-end pipeline from raw contract data to risk score
pipeline.step1Desc: Each of the 16 features is normalized relative to its sector and year baseline, producing z-scores that measure how unusual a contract is compared to peers.
pipeline.step2Desc: The z-score vector is evaluated against the sector covariance matrix (Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage) to detect multivariate anomalies — unusual combinations of factors.
pipeline.step3Desc: Optuna-tuned ElasticNet (C=0.0100, l1_ratio=0.9673) with 12 per-sector sub-models trained on ~288K known-bad contracts from ~748 corruption cases converts z-scores into risk scores.
pipeline.step4Desc: Elkan & Noto (2008) holdout PU-learning correction (c=0.300) adjusts for unlabeled corrupt contracts. Bootstrap (500 resamples) provides 95% confidence intervals.

evolution.title: Model Evolution
evolution.subtitle: From expert-weighted checklist (v3.3) to per-sector ML with cross-model validation and explainability (v5.2 layer).

steps.v33Desc: 8 expert-weighted factors, IMF CRI aligned. AUC barely above random.
steps.v40Desc: Z-score normalization, Bayesian calibration, 9 corruption cases, 12 features.
steps.v50Desc: 12 sector sub-models, 15 documented cases, temporal train/test split.
steps.v51Desc: 22 cases including SAT EFOS Case 22: 38 RFC-confirmed ghost companies.
steps.v60Desc: ~748 cases, ~603 vendors. Vendor-stratified split (no vendor in both train+test), time-windowed labels, Optuna TPE.
steps.v52Desc: SHAP per-vendor explanations (456K vendors) + PyOD cross-model ensemble (9.3M scores) + KS drift monitoring. See Vendor Profile → Risk tab, Contract detail → AI Confirmed, Dashboard → Cross-Model.

overview.p1End: with 95% confidence intervals. The v0.6.5 model uses vendor-stratified validation (no vendor appears in both training and test sets), producing an honest test AUC of 0.828. A score of 0.35 means this contract's procurement characteristics closely resemble those from documented corruption cases.
overview.formulaDescStart: Where z = z-score features, beta = learned coefficients, sigma = logistic sigmoid, c = PU correction (0.300). S(z) is a
overview.formulaDescEnd: — it measures how closely a contract's procurement characteristics resemble those from documented corruption cases. It is not a literal probability of corruption.
overview.highRiskSummaryEnd: (critical + high). Thresholds calibrated against ~748 documented cases to yield a rate consistent with international procurement risk ranges. v0.6.5 is the active model (Mar 25, 2026), using vendor-stratified validation.

features.p1: Each contract is described by 16 z-score features normalized by sector and year baselines. v0.6.5 uses 4 behavioral features added in v5.0: price_volatility, institution_diversity, win_rate, and sector_spread. The chart below shows the learned cross-validated ElasticNet coefficients. Positive coefficients increase the risk score; negative coefficients decrease it.
features.footerNote: Coefficients from Optuna-tuned ElasticNet (C=0.0100, l1_ratio=0.9673, near-pure L1). Near-L1 regularization drives uninformative features to zero. Two features (co_bid_rate, price_hyp_confidence) are regularized to exactly zero across all 13 models.

riskEvidence.p1: Each risk factor is grounded in peer-reviewed literature on procurement corruption. The table below shows each factor's learned coefficient, global evidence strength, and key source. Hover over a factor name for mechanism details.

findings.finding1Title: Price Volatility is the strongest predictor (+0.53)
findings.finding1Body: Vendors with wildly varying contract sizes remain the strongest corruption signal. This is followed by vendor concentration (+0.37) and institution diversity (-0.38, protective). Price ratio (+0.23) and network membership (+0.18) are meaningful positive predictors. Win rate is a weak positive signal (+0.05) in v0.6.5.
findings.finding2Title: Key sign changes from v5.1 to v0.6.5
findings.finding2Item1End: — Win rate collapsed from a strong predictor to near-zero. Many GT vendors receive direct awards rather than winning competitive bids, making win_rate a confounded signal.
findings.finding2Item2End: — In v5.1, cross-sector operation was protective. In v0.6.5, large GT vendors like Edenred and Toka operate across sectors, causing the model to associate sector spread with corruption. This is a labeling artifact.
findings.finding2Item3End: — Single bidding provides no discriminating power in v0.6.5. Known-bad vendors tend to win through direct awards rather than single-bid competitive procedures, so this classic OECD indicator has no signal in Mexican federal procurement ground truth.
findings.finding3BodyEnd: — The co_bid_rate coefficient was regularized to exactly 0.0 by L2 regularization. Co-bidding patterns do not help distinguish known-bad contracts from random contracts in this dataset. This suggests the known-bad vendors operate through market concentration rather than collusion.
findings.finding4Strong: These findings challenge the standard OECD/IMF anti-corruption framework
findings.finding4End: for Mexican procurement. The traditional indicators (single bidding, direct awards, short ad periods) that dominate international methodology are weak or reversed in our ground truth data. This may reflect the specific characteristics of Mexican procurement corruption, or the limitations of our ground truth sample.

validation.p1Start: The model was trained and validated against
validation.p1End: , matching ~603 vendors to ~288K contracts across all 12 sectors in the COMPRANET database.
validation.footnote: *Case 9 (PEMEX Emilio Lozoya) shares vendors with the Odebrecht case. Documented for reference but does not contribute additional training data. The table above shows the original 22 foundation cases from v5.1. v0.6.5 expanded the ground truth to ~748 cases and ~603 vendors through systematic COMPRANET investigation, ASF audit cross-referencing, and SAT EFOS integration.

methods.zScoreP1: Each feature is normalized relative to its sector and year baseline. A direct award in Defensa (where 80% are direct) is less suspicious than in Educacion (where 50% are direct).
methods.zScoreNote: 3,372 baselines computed across 12 sectors, ~24 years, and 16 features. Fallback hierarchy: sector+year (if n >= 30), sector-only (if n >= 100), global.
methods.mahalanobisP1: Captures multivariate anomalies — contracts that are unusual across multiple dimensions simultaneously. This replaces the 5 hardcoded interaction pairs from v3.3 with a full covariance-based approach.
methods.mahalanobisNote: Covariance estimated with Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage for stability in small sectors. P-values computed against chi-squared distribution with 16 degrees of freedom.
methods.logisticP1: Optuna-tuned ElasticNet (C=0.0100, l1_ratio=0.9673, near-pure L1) trained on ~288K known-bad contracts from ~748 corruption cases with vendor-stratified 70/30 split (no vendor in both train and test). 12 per-sector sub-models + 1 global fallback capture sector-specific corruption patterns.
methods.logisticNote: Train AUC: 0.798, Test AUC: 0.828. Near-L1 regularization drives uninformative features to exactly zero, producing a sparser and more robust model than v5.1.
methods.puP1Start: Since unlabeled contracts are not necessarily clean (some may be corrupt but undetected), we apply a Positive-Unlabeled learning correction:
methods.puNote: v0.6.5 estimated c = 0.300 via Elkan & Noto holdout on 13 models (1 global + 12 per-sector). This reflects the heterogeneity of the expanded ground truth (~603 vendors with diverse patterns). With c=0.300, scores are rescaled by ~3.3x and capped at 1.0. IMPORTANT: The SCAR assumption is structurally violated — known cases are high-profile scandals, not a random sample of all corruption.
methods.bootstrapP1: Each contract receives a 95% confidence interval from 500 bootstrap resamples of the training data. A score of 0.35 [0.22, 0.48] means: the risk indicator is 0.35, but given data uncertainty, it could be as low as 0.22 or as high as 0.48.

v52layer.p1End: adds three independent ML tools on top of the active v0.6.5 risk scores. These tools do not replace or alter any contract risk scores — they provide additional explanations and cross-validation signals.
v52layer.shapBody: Exact Shapley values (φᵢ = βᵢ × (zᵢ − E[zᵢ])) computed for each vendor using their sector-specific model coefficients. Shows which of the 16 z-score features drove the risk score up or down.
v52layer.shapWhere: Vendor Profile → Risk tab → SHAP Analysis panel
v52layer.pyodBody: Isolation Forest + COPOD ensemble anomaly detection run independently on all 3.05M contracts. A high ensemble score means the contract is a statistical outlier even without knowing the ground truth label.
v52layer.pyodWhere: Contract detail → AI Confirmed badge · Dashboard → Cross-Model Validation · Explore → AI Confirmed filter
v52layer.driftBody: Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests compare the 2024–2025 feature distribution against the 2002–2020 training baseline. Detects when new procurement data develops patterns unlike the training period.
v52layer.aiConfirmedEnd: when the supervised v0.6.5 logistic model scores it as high or critical risk and the unsupervised PyOD ensemble independently scores it above the 0.50 anomaly threshold. Two completely different methods — one trained on labeled corruption cases, one trained on no labels at all — agree that this contract is unusual. This convergence reduces the false-positive rate for investigation triage.
v52layer.noteStart: Note: v5.2 is an analytical overlay — it adds explanability and cross-validation. The active risk model is v0.6.5. Contract
v52layer.noteEnd: values are unchanged by the v5.2 layer.

limitations.item1Text: v0.6.5 expanded to ~748 cases and ~603 vendors (~288K training contracts) with a per-vendor cap of 100. While this dramatically reduces the dominance of mega-cases, Salud and Agricultura sectors still contribute a disproportionate share of positive training data due to the IMSS, Segalmex, and DICONSA/LICONSA ecosystems.
limitations.item2Text: Cases with few contracts (La Estafa Maestra: 10, Grupo Higa: 3, Oceanografia: 2) have lower detection rates. The model requires sufficient contract volume to detect patterns reliably.
limitations.item3Text: Structure A (2002-2010) has only 0.1% RFC coverage. Z-scores and risk estimates are less reliable for this period. Network analysis requires 2010+ data for meaningful results.
limitations.item4Text: The PU correction (c=0.300) reflects the heterogeneity of expanded GT — many newly added vendors have ambiguous patterns. The SCAR assumption is structurally violated: known cases are high-profile scandals, not a random sample of all corruption.
limitations.item5Text: The co_bid_rate coefficient was regularized to exactly 0.0 in both the global model and all 12 per-sector sub-models. Co-bidding patterns do not discriminate in our current ground truth.
limitations.item6Text: A high risk score indicates a statistical anomaly consistent with documented corruption patterns. It does not constitute proof of wrongdoing. Risk scores are intended to prioritize investigation, not determine guilt.

v33section.p1End: with 8 base factors aligned to the IMF Corruption Risk Index methodology. Each factor contributed a percentage of the total score, with interaction bonuses for correlated factor pairs. Scores were preserved in the
v33section.bonusFactors: Additional bonus factors: Co-bidding +5%, Price Hypothesis +5%, Industry Mismatch +3%, Institution Risk +3%. 5 interaction pairs with up to +15% bonus. Score capped at 1.0.

dataSources.p1End: , the Mexican government's official electronic procurement system. The data spans 4 different structures with varying column counts and quality levels.
dataSources.rejectEnd: Contracts exceeding 100B MXN are excluded as data entry errors (decimal point mistakes in original COMPRANET data).
dataSources.flagEnd: Contracts between 10B and 100B MXN are included but marked for manual review.
dataSources.context: Context: Mexico's entire federal budget is ~8T MXN annually. A 100B contract would be 1.25% of the national budget.

references.footer: Risk scores are statistical indicators with confidence intervals. A high score indicates similarity to documented corruption patterns -- it does not constitute proof of wrongdoing.
